title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing a new brand of 'functional' mushrooms being sold as coffee substitutes.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I saw you drinking that 'mushroom brew' earlier. Is it actually any good or is it just another wellness fad? Woman: [1]I was skeptical too, but I’ve found that it gives me a much steadier stream of energy than caffeine[/1]. I don't get those afternoon jitters anymore. Man: Interesting. I’ve heard they’re supposed to be 'adaptogens', whatever that means. Woman: Basically, they help your body manage stress. Though [2]I have to say, the earthy aftertaste takes some getting used to[/2]. You definitely can't drink it black. Man: I think I'll stick to my espresso. I like my coffee to taste like, well, coffee. Woman: Fair enough. But if you ever find yourself struggling with sleep because of the caffeine, it’s worth a shot.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I saw you drinking that 'mushroom brew' earlier. Is it actually any good or is it just another wellness fad?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I was skeptical too, but I’ve found that it gives me a much steadier stream of energy than caffeine. I don't get those afternoon jitters anymore.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Interesting. I’ve heard they’re supposed to be 'adaptogens', whatever that means.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Basically, they help your body manage stress. Though I have to say, the earthy aftertaste takes some getting used to. You definitely can't drink it black.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I think I'll stick to my espresso. I like my coffee to taste like, well, coffee.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Fair enough. But if you ever find yourself struggling with sleep because of the caffeine, it’s worth a shot.

questions.question: What is the woman's main reason for switching to the mushroom drink?

options.A: It is significantly cheaper than regular coffee.
options.B: It provides a more consistent level of alertness.
options.C: It has a more pleasant flavor profile.

questions.explanation: She mentions a 'steadier stream of energy' and the absence of 'jitters', indicating consistent alertness without the spikes of caffeine.

questions.question: What does the woman admit about the drink?

options.A: It is difficult to find in local shops.
options.B: It requires special equipment to prepare.
options.C: Its flavor can be initially unappealing.

questions.explanation: She notes the 'earthy aftertaste takes some getting used to', suggesting it isn't immediately likeable.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing the rise of 'minimalist' living.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’ve finally started decluttering the attic. It’s amazing how much junk you can accumulate in ten years. Woman: I went through that last summer. [3]It’s quite a psychological hurdle, though, isn't it? Deciding what defines you and what’s just 'stuff'[/3]. Man: Absolutely. I found myself getting weirdly sentimental over an old broken toaster. Woman: [4]The danger is that 'minimalism' itself becomes a form of consumerism[/4]. People throw everything out only to buy expensive, 'minimalist-looking' replacements. Man: True. I’m trying to avoid that trap. I just want some breathing room. Woman: I find the hardest part is maintaining it. The 'stuff' has a way of creeping back in when you aren't looking.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’ve finally started decluttering the attic. It’s amazing how much junk you can accumulate in ten years.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I went through that last summer. It’s quite a psychological hurdle, though, isn't it? Deciding what defines you and what’s just 'stuff'

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Absolutely. I found myself getting weirdly sentimental over an old broken toaster.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: The danger is that 'minimalism' itself becomes a form of consumerism. People throw everything out only to buy expensive, 'minimalist-looking' replacements.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: True. I’m trying to avoid that trap. I just want some breathing room.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I find the hardest part is maintaining it. The 'stuff' has a way of creeping back in when you aren't looking.

questions.question: The woman suggests that the main difficulty of decluttering is...

options.A: the physical effort involved in moving heavy items.
options.B: the emotional challenge of re-evaluating one's possessions.
options.C: the time required to organize everything effectively.

questions.explanation: She calls it a 'psychological hurdle' and mentions deciding 'what defines you', which is an emotional and identity-based process.

questions.question: What irony does the woman point out regarding the minimalism trend?

options.B: It can lead to a lack of practical household items.
options.C: It is usually practiced by those with the most possessions.

questions.explanation: She states that minimalism can become a 'form of consumerism' where people buy 'expensive replacements'.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, Connor and Emily, discussing their company's new policy on using personal phones for work.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Did you see the memo about the 'Bring Your Own Device' scheme? Woman: I did. [5]I’m not sure I’m comfortable with the IT department having the ability to 'remote-wipe' my personal phone[/5] if I lose it. Man: I get that, but they say it’s purely for security reasons—to protect client data. Woman: I know, but [6]it further blurs the line between my private life and my job[/6]. I don't want to be thinking about spreadsheets every time I check my personal messages. Man: They are offering a small monthly stipend to cover the data costs, though. Woman: That’s peanuts compared to the loss of privacy. I think I’ll just stick to using my laptop for everything.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Did you see the memo about the 'Bring Your Own Device' scheme?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I did. I’m not sure I’m comfortable with the IT department having the ability to 'remote-wipe' my personal phone if I lose it.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I get that, but they say it’s purely for security reasons—to protect client data.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I know, but it further blurs the line between my private life and my job. I don't want to be thinking about spreadsheets every time I check my personal messages.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: They are offering a small monthly stipend to cover the data costs, though.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: That’s peanuts compared to the loss of privacy. I think I’ll just stick to using my laptop for everything.

questions.question: What is the woman's primary technical concern about the new scheme?

options.A: The potential for her data to be stolen by hackers.
options.B: The level of control the company would have over her hardware.
options.C: The incompatibility of her phone with work software.

questions.explanation: She mentions being uncomfortable with the IT department's ability to 'remote-wipe' her phone, which is a form of hardware control.

questions.question: The woman objects to the scheme because she believes it will...

options.A: make it harder to disconnect from work during her free time.
options.B: lead to her phone becoming obsolete more quickly.

questions.explanation: She says it 'blurs the line between private life and job' and she doesn't want to think of work when checking personal messages.
